skill.name: video-inpainting
skill.description: Guides video region edits through the RunComfy CLI, including object removal, watermark cleanup, and prompt-driven replacements across frames.
skill.summary: Edit or remove regions across video frames using RunComfy video inpainting routes.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily over-reported risk because Markdown backticks, SAM2 segmentation references, and model names were misclassified as shell execution, credential theft, Windows SAM access, and weak cryptography. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the skill authorizes RunComfy CLI commands, sends user-provided video inputs to external RunComfy services, and documents local token storage. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, arbitrary shell execution, or unauthorized credential access.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash commands matching runcomfy and gives examples that install, authenticate, and run the RunComfy CLI. This is expected for the skill, but it can execute external tooling and should be used only with the verified CLI and explicit user inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples directly authorize RunComfy CLI use. The scope is limited to runcomfy commands, so the risk is real but bounded.

medium_findings.title: User Video Data Sent to External RunComfy Services
medium_findings.description: The workflow uses a video URL and states that the CLI posts to the Model API, polls status, and downloads results. This is normal for a hosted video editing service, but users must know that video inputs and prompts leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicitly documented and tied to the service function. No evidence indicates data is sent to unrelated endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Local Token Storage and Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill documents token storage under the user configuration directory and uses output directories for generated video downloads. This creates normal credential and filesystem exposure risks if permissions or output locations are mishandled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and token storage behavior are described directly. The documentation also states mode 0600 and a 2 GiB download cap, which reduces but does not remove risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most static external command detections are Markdown inline code spans or fenced examples, not Ruby backtick execution. The confirmed executable surface is limited to the documented RunComfy CLI usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown, and the flagged text appears inside code formatting or command examples. There is no Ruby source or dynamic backtick execution.

low_findings.title: SAM2 Segmentation Misclassified as Windows SAM Access
low_findings.description: The critical Windows SAM findings refer to SAM2 segmentation tracking for video masks, not the Windows Security Account Manager database. No evidence found of Windows credential database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text discusses video segmentation and mask propagation. It does not reference Windows paths, registry hives, or credential extraction.

low_findings.title: Model and Workflow Terms Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography hits are caused by model names or ordinary prose, not cryptographic API use. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or insecure cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are headings, descriptions, or prompt guidance. They contain no cryptographic libraries, algorithms, or security-sensitive computations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Obfuscation Heuristics Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined legitimate CLI, network, and token documentation into suspicious heuristics. Manual review found no system reconnaissance, obfuscation, credential harvesting, or prompt injection instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines describe model limitations and security guidance for the RunComfy CLI. The combination is explainable by the skill purpose, though external service use still warrants a medium risk warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allowance for RunComfy Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands. This should remain restricted to the RunComfy CLI namespace and should not be expanded without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool allowance is explicit in the front matter and is the central execution boundary for the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted API Processing of User Media
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends media references and prompts to RunComfy hosted services for processing. Users should avoid sending videos that they are not authorized to process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly depends on outbound hosted model calls. The endpoint allowlist reduces ambiguity about intended destinations.

content.value_statement: Video region cleanup is difficult when objects move across frames. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose RunComfy routes for prompt-driven video inpainting.
actual_capabilities: Selects Wan 2-7 Edit-Video as the default route for prompt-based region edits.
Provides RunComfy CLI examples for submitting a source video and edit prompt.
Compares Wan, Lucy Edit Restyle, and Seedream routes for different video editing needs.
Explains when pixel-precise mask propagation requires a ComfyUI workflow instead of the CLI.
Documents security guidance for CLI installation, token handling, endpoint allowlists, and output limits.
limitations: It does not perform local video editing by itself; it guides RunComfy CLI usage.
Prompt-driven edits may not provide exact pixel-level control across every frame.
Long clips and complex motion can reduce temporal consistency.
The workflow requires a RunComfy account, network access, and suitable source video URLs.

use_cases.title: Clean Product Demo Footage
use_cases.description: Remove small labels, wires, or background distractions from short clips while preserving the main product motion.

use_cases.title: Prepare Social Video Revisions
use_cases.description: Replace or remove visible objects across a clip using spatial prompts and RunComfy video edit routes.

use_cases.title: Prototype Video Repair Workflows
use_cases.description: Decide when to use RunComfy CLI endpoints and when to move to ComfyUI mask propagation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Remove a Simple Object
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner cleanup on a short clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the video-inpainting skill to remove the small object in the lower right of this clip. Preserve all other content and explain the RunComfy route you choose.

prompt_templates.title: Clean a Watermark Across Frames
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me remove the watermark from this video across all frames. Use spatial language, choose the best RunComfy model, and include the key CLI inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Editing Routes
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose between prompt edit, restyle, and frame-stack treatment
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether Wan 2-7 Edit-Video, Lucy Edit Restyle, or Seedream Edit-Sequential fits this requested region edit. Explain tradeoffs and risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Mask-Driven Repair
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for precise target tracking
prompt_templates.prompt: The edit needs precise mask propagation across moving frames. Use this skill to decide whether the CLI is enough or a ComfyUI workflow is required.

output_examples.input: Remove a logo from the bottom-right corner of a short product video.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Wan 2-7 Edit-Video, writes a preservation-focused prompt, and notes that mask-based ComfyUI workflows may improve difficult cases.

output_examples.input: Replace a moving object while keeping the same subject identity.
output_examples.output: The skill compares Lucy Edit Restyle with Wan 2-7, explains identity stability concerns, and suggests splitting compound edits into separate passes.

output_examples.input: Repair a clip where the target crosses behind foreground objects.
output_examples.output: The skill flags prompt-only editing as risky and points toward mask propagation with a dedicated ComfyUI workflow.

best_practices: Describe the target region with clear spatial language and one requested change per run.
Use only video URLs that the user explicitly provides and is authorized to process.
Start with short clips, review artifacts, and move to mask-driven workflows when precision matters.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the model to perform multiple unrelated edits in one pass.
Do not use arbitrary remote install scripts for the RunComfy CLI.
Do not send private or third-party videos to hosted services without permission.

faq.question: Does this skill edit videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides RunComfy CLI and RunComfy-hosted workflows for video region edits.

faq.question: Which model is the default route?
faq.answer: The skill uses Wan 2-7 Edit-Video as the default for prompt-driven region edits.

faq.question: Can it use exact masks?
faq.answer: The CLI routes are prompt-driven. Exact mask propagation usually needs a ComfyUI workflow.

faq.question: What data leaves my machine?
faq.answer: The source video reference, prompt, and request metadata are sent to RunComfy services.

faq.question: Does it support watermark removal?
faq.answer: It can guide watermark cleanup, but users must have the legal right to remove the mark.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses an external CLI, network processing, token storage, and output file downloads.
